content.text: Azure Portal settings (gear icon, top-right) let each user customise their portal experience. Settings are stored per Microsoft account and synchronise across devices and browsers automatically. They do not affect other users, do not change Azure resource configuration, and take effect immediately without a page reload in most cases.

attrs.alt: Azure Portal settings panel showing Appearance & startup views, Theme options, Startup page, Menu behaviour, Inactivity timeout, Language/Region, and Directories/Subscriptions

content.text: Portal settings are separate from Azure resource configuration. They control only how the portal looks and behaves for your account.

# Access portal settings:
1. Click the gear (⚙) icon in the top navigation bar
   OR
2. Navigate directly to:
   https://portal.azure.com/#settings/hub

# Settings are organised into five sections:
- Appearance + startup views   (theme, startup page, menu, timeout)
- Directories + subscriptions  (which tenants/subscriptions are visible)
- Language + region            (UI language and date/number format)
- My information               (contact preferences, feedback)
- Signing out + notifications  (session behaviour, push notifications)
The Appearance section controls the visual theme, the page shown when you first open the portal, and how the left sidebar behaves.
Black background, white text, yellow accents
Visual accessibility, WCAG AA contrast requirements
White background, black text, blue accents

High Contrast themes also improve keyboard navigation visibility — the focused element's outline is much more distinct than in the standard themes. Enable High Contrast if you find it difficult to track keyboard focus.
The startup page determines what you see when you open portal.azure.com. The default is Home (the portal landing page with recent resources). You can change it to any specific dashboard.

# Change startup page:
Settings > Appearance + startup views > Startup page
Options:
  Home           — portal home with recent resources and services
  Dashboard      — opens the last-used dashboard
  <Named dashboard> — opens a specific shared or private dashboard

# If you set startup to a specific dashboard that is later deleted,
# the portal falls back to Home automatically.

# To set startup to a dashboard via portal settings:
1. Settings (gear) > Appearance + startup views
2. Under "Startup page" > select "Dashboard"
3. Under "Dashboard" dropdown > select the named dashboard
4. Click "Apply"

# Two sidebar menu modes:
Flyout (default): sidebar is collapsed to icons; hover or click to expand
Docked:           sidebar is always expanded showing full service names

# When to use Docked:
- Wide monitors where sidebar space is not a concern
- When learning; seeing full service names helps orientation
- Accessibility: easier to navigate without needing to hover

# Change via:
Settings > Appearance + startup views > Menu behaviour
Select "Flyout" or "Docked" > Apply
The inactivity timeout signs you out of the portal after a period of no activity. This is an important security setting for shared or unattended machines.
Session stays active until you sign out or close the browser
Personal laptop with disk encryption enabled
Signs out after 1 minute with no mouse or keyboard activity
Signs out after 5 minutes of inactivity
Signs out after 15 minutes of inactivity
Signs out after 30 minutes of inactivity
Signs out after 1 hour of inactivity
Long-running monitoring tasks where you step away

The inactivity timeout in portal settings is per-account and per-browser. If your organisation enforces a sign-in frequency via Azure AD Conditional Access policies, that policy supersedes the portal's own timeout — the portal timeout is the minimum of the two.
This section filters which Azure AD directories (tenants) and subscriptions appear throughout the portal. It does not grant or revoke access — it only hides or shows subscriptions in the portal UI.
Your account has guest access to many customer tenants via Azure Lighthouse or B2B — hide all but the active one to reduce noise
You manage dozens of subscriptions but primarily work in three — show only those three to keep resource lists manageable
You are troubleshooting a specific subscription — showing only that subscription prevents accidental changes to others

# Filter directories and subscriptions:
Settings (gear) > Directories + subscriptions

Under "Directories":
  - Lists all tenants your account is a member or guest of
  - Toggle each on/off; only enabled tenants appear in the portal

Under "Default subscription filter":
  - Shows all subscriptions in the active directory
  - Tick/untick to show/hide each subscription
  - "All subscriptions" toggle at top selects/deselects all

Under "Advanced filters":
  - Filter by subscription state (Enabled, Disabled, Past Due)
  - Filter by offer type (Pay-As-You-Go, EA, Free Trial, etc.)

# After applying: the portal reloads and shows only selected subscriptions.
# CLI is unaffected — CLI context is set separately with:
az account set --subscription "sub-name-or-id"

# Two ways to switch the active directory:

Method 1: Portal Settings
Settings > Directories + subscriptions > Switch

Method 2: Account menu
Click your avatar (top-right) > Switch directory > select tenant

# After switching, the portal reloads.
# Your Favourites, recently visited resources, and dashboards
# may differ between directories.

# If a directory does not appear in the list:
- You may not be a member or guest of that tenant
- Request an invitation from the tenant's Azure AD admin
- Or: your account may need MFA claims — sign in via:
  https://portal.azure.com/<tenant-domain>
Language controls the portal UI text. Regional format controls how dates, times, and numbers are displayed in the portal — independently of the UI language.

# Settings > Language + region

Language:
  - 50+ languages supported
  - Changes all portal UI labels, tooltips, and help text
  - Does NOT change the language of Azure resource names or CLI output

Regional format:
  - Controls date format (MM/DD/YYYY vs DD/MM/YYYY)
  - Controls time format (12h vs 24h)
  - Controls number format (1,000.00 vs 1.000,00)
  - Can be set independently of UI language
  - Example: UI in English but dates in UK format (DD/MM/YYYY)

# After changing language or regional format:
Click "Apply" and then "Refresh" — portal reloads with the new locale.

# Portal language does NOT affect:
- Azure CLI output language (set with AZURE_CORE_LANGUAGE env var)
- ARM API responses (always in English)
- Azure resource names or descriptions
The Signing out + notifications section controls session behaviour and optional push notification preferences.
Sign me out when browser closes
Clears session tokens when the browser is closed; prevents persistent login on shared machines
Controls the in-portal bell notification count for long-running operations
Browser push notifications for Azure operations (requires browser permission grant)
The smiley-face feedback icon in the bottom-right of the portal

# Sign-out behaviour:
Settings > Signing out + notifications > Sign me out when browser closes

# When enabled: closing the browser tab or window signs you out.
# When disabled: session persists until the inactivity timeout or explicit sign-out.

# Recommended for shared computers:
- Enable "Sign me out when browser closes"
- Set inactivity timeout to 5 or 15 minutes
- Use a private/incognito window as an additional precaution

# Explicit sign-out:
Avatar (top-right) > Sign out
OR navigate to: https://portal.azure.com/#@domain/signout

The My information section shows the account details for the currently signed-in Microsoft account and links to the Microsoft account profile page. It is read-only in the portal — changes to the account profile (display name, contact email, phone number) are made at account.microsoft.com or the Azure AD user profile.

# View account profile via CLI
az ad signed-in-user show \
  --query '{displayName:displayName, upn:userPrincipalName, id:id, jobTitle:jobTitle}' \
  -o table

# List directories (tenants) your account can access
az account tenant list -o table

# Update your own Azure AD profile attributes (requires appropriate permissions)
az ad user update \
  --id jane.smith@contoso.com \
  --job-title "Senior Engineer" \
  --department "Platform Engineering"
Portal settings can be reset to their defaults at any time. This is useful after experimenting with settings or when the portal behaves unexpectedly after configuration changes.

# Reset appearance settings:
Settings > Appearance + startup views > "Reset to default"
This resets: theme (Azure light), startup page (Home), menu (Flyout), timeout (Never)

# Reset directory/subscription filter:
Settings > Directories + subscriptions > "Select all" under subscriptions
This makes all accessible subscriptions visible again.

# Reset language:
Settings > Language + region > Language: "English (United States)" > Apply

# Clear cached portal state (for deeper resets):
1. Open portal in a private/incognito browser window
2. Or clear browser cache and cookies for portal.azure.com
3. The portal reloads with fresh defaults
Portal settings are stored in Azure against your Microsoft account identity, not in the browser. This means settings follow you across devices and browsers when you sign in with the same account.
Theme, startup page, menu behaviour, inactivity timeout
Browser zoom level and window size

Portal settings (theme, startup page, favourites) are stored in the Microsoft.Portal namespace. While most users configure them through the UI, administrators and onboarding scripts can read these settings via ARM and the Portal REST API.
Reading Portal User Settings via REST

# Portal user settings are stored per-user at:
# GET https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Portal/usersettings/default?api-version=2020-09-01-preview

TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)

curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Portal/usersettings/default?api-version=2020-09-01-preview" \
  | python3 -m json.tool

# Response includes:
# properties.content.environment: theme setting
# properties.content.pinnedExtensions: pinned sidebar extensions
# properties.content.startupPage: startup page configuration

# Dashboards are ARM resources under Microsoft.Portal/dashboards
# List all dashboards across all accessible subscriptions
az resource list \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards \
  --query "[].{Name:name, RG:resourceGroup, Sub:subscriptionId, Location:location}" \
  -o table

# Get a specific dashboard's properties
az resource show \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards \
  --name ops-dashboard-prod \
  -o json | python3 -m json.tool

# Export dashboard JSON for version control
az resource show \
  --resource-group rg-shared-ops \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Portal/dashboards \
  --name ops-dashboard-prod \
  --query properties \
  -o json > ops-dashboard-prod-backup.json

Azure AD Conditional Access policies can impose additional requirements on portal sign-in beyond the standard username/password. Understanding these policies prevents unexpected login failures when using the portal in new environments.
Portal prompts for a second factor (authenticator app, SMS, phone)
Complete MFA prompt or pre-register MFA methods at aka.ms/mfasetup
Login blocked if device is not enrolled in Intune
Enrol device in Intune or use a corporate-managed device
Login blocked from IP ranges outside allowed network
Use VPN to route through a permitted IP range
Portal forces re-authentication after N hours regardless of activity
Accept the re-auth prompt; portal state is preserved after re-login
Session cookie not persisted; must sign in again on each browser open
Expected behaviour; store work in git/portal templates not in the browser

# Check which Conditional Access policies apply to your account (requires admin)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/conditionalAccess/policies" \
  --headers "Content-Type=application/json" \
  --query "value[].{Name:displayName, State:state, Users:conditions.users}" \
  -o table

# As a regular user: check your sign-in history in Azure AD
# Azure AD > Users > your account > Sign-in logs
# Each entry shows which CA policies were evaluated and their result
Portal in Private Browsing and Incognito Mode
The portal works in private/incognito mode, but some behaviours differ. Understanding these differences prevents confusion when using incognito for security isolation.
Session cookies persist until timeout or sign-out
Session ends when incognito window closes
Loaded from Azure account (same as any other session)
Loaded from Azure account (settings follow the account, not the browser)
Shown on home page (stored in Azure, not the browser)
Browser cache speeds up portal loads
First load slower; no cache benefit

If the portal behaves unexpectedly (blank blades, authentication loops, broken tiles), opening it in a private/incognito window is the fastest diagnostic step — it rules out browser extension interference and stale cache as causes.
Enforcing Portal Settings at the Tenant Level
Azure AD administrators can enforce some portal behaviours for all users in the tenant via Azure AD settings and portal administration settings. These override individual user preferences.
Disabling the Azure Portal for Non-Admins

# Restrict Azure portal access (Azure AD Portal Administration settings)
# Navigate to: Azure AD > User settings > Restrict access to Azure AD administration portal

# Via Microsoft Graph API:
az rest \
  --method PATCH \
  --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authorizationPolicy" \
  --body '{
    "defaultUserRolePermissions": {
      "allowedToCreateApps": false,
      "allowedToCreateSecurityGroups": false,
      "allowedToCreateTenants": false,
      "allowedToReadBitlockerKeysForOwnedDevice": true,
      "allowedToReadOtherUsers": true
    }
  }' \
  --headers "Content-Type=application/json"

# Note: this restricts Azure AD admin pages but not the ARM portal.
# To block ARM portal access: use a Conditional Access policy targeting
# the "Microsoft Azure Management" application (ID: 797f4846-ba00-4fd7-ba43-dac1f8f63013)
The Azure Portal has built-in accessibility features beyond the High Contrast themes. Understanding these helps operators and administrators support team members with accessibility needs.
Keyboard navigation: every interactive element is reachable with Tab/Shift+Tab, activated with Enter/Space, and dismissed with Escape
Screen reader support: the portal generates ARIA landmarks and labels compatible with NVDA (Windows), JAWS (Windows), and VoiceOver (macOS)
Focus indicators: visible focus rings on all focused elements (more prominent in High Contrast themes)
Text resize: the portal respects browser text size settings up to 200% without horizontal scrolling
Reduced motion: the portal respects the operating system's 'Reduce motion' accessibility setting to minimise animations
Colour-blind modes: High Contrast themes avoid colour-only encoding of information

# Enable High Contrast (portal setting):
Settings (gear) > Appearance + startup views > Theme: High Contrast Dark

# Enable High Contrast (operating system - takes precedence):
Windows: Settings > Accessibility > High Contrast > turn on
macOS: System Settings > Accessibility > Display > Increase Contrast

# Screen reader tips for the Azure Portal:
- Use NVDA's "Browse mode" (Insert+Space) to navigate by headings (H key)
- ARIA landmarks: use the Landmarks list (NVDA: Insert+F7) to jump to main content
- Blade titles are announced as H1 landmarks
- Lists of resources are rendered as ARIA grids with row and column headers

Azure Portal settings (gear icon) are per-account preferences stored in Azure and synchronised across all devices. The five main sections are Appearance + startup views (theme, startup page, menu mode, inactivity timeout), Directories + subscriptions (which tenants and subscriptions the portal shows), Language + region (UI text language and date/number formats), My information (account profile link), and Signing out + notifications (session and push notification controls). None of these settings affect Azure resource configuration — they only change how the portal looks and behaves for your account.
